I am opposed to removing all of the imports from `__init.py__`.  Before you 🤬, lemme explain my thinking.

With an empty `__init.py__`, if someone wants to use the `AddSubmenu` decorator, they must first import it using `from cmd2.cmd2 import AddSubmenu`. If we decide to move that decorator into it's own source code file called `submenu.py`, then we break everyone's imports.

When we include import statements in `__init.py__` we shield users of `cmd2` from how we have organized our classes in the source code files. This is an important part of providing a stable external interface to our library.

I see the value in the quick startup time for integrating with bash completion. @anselor states that he observes "about half a second lag" when he hits the tab key on a shell command utilizing the capability. That's clearly too slow. I thought I would try and figure out if there is a way to keep a nice set of imports in `__init.py__` and still have speedy startup times.

As always, performance measurement is difficult to replicate, but measurement can help pinpoint potential problems. Here's some data I collected on my 2015 MacBook Pro.
```
$ time python -c ""

real	0m0.106s
user	0m0.057s
sys	0m0.043s
```

I use `pyenv` which shims every python executable. If I skip the shimming I can save 60 ms or so:
```
$ time ~/.pyenv/versions/cmd2-3.6/bin/python -c ""

real	0m0.038s
user	0m0.028s
sys	0m0.010s
```
That's pretty fast. There is some variation (maybe 10%) but it's generally around 40 ms. With an empty `__init.py__`, importing `cmd2` there is no appreciable difference in speed:
```
time ~/.pyenv/versions/cmd2-3.6/bin/python -c "import cmd2"

real	0m0.037s
user	0m0.025s
sys	0m0.010s
```

When `__init.py__` imports Cmd with `from .cmd2 import Cmd', the times go up:
```
time ~/.pyenv/versions/cmd2-3.6/bin/python -c "import cmd2"

real	0m0.157s
user	0m0.112s
sys	0m0.038s
```

On my machine, it takes ~120ms to import `cmd2`. I went through `cmd2.py` to see if I could figure out what the slow parts of importing were. It's death by a thousand cuts. Well, not quite a thousand, but we import 23(!) modules from the standard library. None of them are individually slow, but combined together it adds up.

Further digging reveals that many of these imported modules are only used in occasional circumstances, for example, the `glob` module is only used when you try and tab complete an executable or a filename, or when running transcript files.

I started commenting out imports to see which ones were not needed at the moment `cmd2` is imported. I identified 14 that could be commented out and still have the import work successfully. The time to import `cmd2` dropped from ~120ms to ~90ms. I believe that with a bit of work which would include moving some code out of `cmd2.py` and into it's own files, and lazy-loading the imports (i.e. instead of running the import at the top of `cmd2.py`, run the import just before using the imported code), we can maybe get to ~70ms to import `cmd2`.

I propose we re-open this issue, and I'll start a branch to see how much I can reduce the import time. After we see what improvements can be made, I suggest we re-evaluate the inclusion of imports in `__init.py__`.

Ah, so just took a closer look at [django-celery-email](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/django-celery-email). Although it *should* work with an Anymail backend in its CELERY_EMAIL_BACKEND setting, by default only the standard Django EmailMessage attributes will make it through. Anymail's custom attributes like `tags` or `template_id` will be lost. The release notes mention a CELERY_EMAIL_MESSAGE_EXTRA_ATTRIBUTES setting that could be used to work around this: set it to a list of Anymail attribute names you use. 

[django-celery-email [manually serializes](https://github.com/pmclanahan/django-celery-email/blob/2.0.0/djcelery_email/backends.py#L15) the EmailMessage objects for the Celery task params, and its [custom serializer](https://github.com/pmclanahan/django-celery-email/blob/2.0.0/djcelery_email/utils.py#L26-L68) only handles known attributes by default. Also, django-celery-mail currently has some [open issues](https://github.com/pmclanahan/django-celery-email/issues/46) around serializing inline attachments and possibly multipart alternatives.]

Speaking of which, if you're on Celery 4.0 or later, your CustomEmailBackend will also need to serialize the email_messages parameter for Celery. [Celery 4's default JSON serialization](http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/latest/userguide/calling.html#calling-serializers) won't handle a Django EmailMessage. The simplest solution would probably be to let Celery do all the work with the `pickle` serializer (which was the default in earlier Celery releases). Pickle can handle a complete EmailMessage object including all custom Anymail additions. Just be aware of potential [security concerns with pickle](http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/latest/userguide/security.html#serializers) if you construct EmailMessages from unsanitized user-supplied content.

Put it all together, and you get something like:

```python
# tasks.py: [UNTESTED CODE]
@app.task(
    autoretry_for=(AnymailAPIError,), retry_backoff=True,  # deal with network glitches
    serializer="pickle")  # handle a list of EmailMessage objects in the param
def async_send_messages(email_messages):
    conn = get_connection(backend='anymail.backends.mailgun.EmailBackend')
    conn.send_messages(email_messages)
```

Re: Performance on a large number of distinct gradients: No, I do not have a test case for that lying around.  Nonetheless, assuming I do not add any bugs in the changeset, the performance for those should improve similarly provided that the total number of gradients.  If you see a speed regression, it will be on SVGs with a low number of gradient - but then the total cost of removeDuplicateGradients will become negligible anyway (as there is simply not a lot of work to do).

The changeset is an optimal CPU time/memory trade-off from an algorithm complexity point of view.  If you double the number of gradients:

 * Original code: The runtime of removeDuplicateGradients basically quad-doubles while memory doubles (i.e. runtime is O(n²) and memory is O(n))

 * Patched code: The runtime and memory usage of removeDuplicateGradients basically doubles (i.e. both are O(n)).  The base amount of memory per gradient is slightly higher compared to the original code.

As I understand it, this memory trade-off is not a concern for scour at the moment, given we load the entire XML into memory.

We've been going back and forth on different ways to resolve this all day, and it's unfortunately very painful.

Our models are provided as pip packages, and in their `__init__.py` they write `import pathlib`. So we can't drop the `pathlib` dependency without the models being broken on Python 2.

This means we can't really use the `pathlib2` module. Our solution is to set a qualifer on the `pathlib` dependency, so it should only install on Python versions before 3.4. This should resolve the problem of `pathlib` shadowing the standard library module.

The magic does not work well because it cannot handle batch execution. The problem is that we only save output for selected cells and cells are not selected during batch execute.

The right way to do this is to cache all output, but there is no appropriate event to catch before or after the execution. Before the execution, the cell [execute function immediately clears output](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/notebook/static/notebook/js/codecell.js#L316) so we cannot wait till the kernel execute function to save output. We cannot do that later either because output can be very slow to appear.

Got this resolved. Before calling `patch_namespaced_deployment`, the body should have annotation field `kubernetes.io/change-cause`. For example,

```
...
with open("/path/to/your/manifest.yml") as f:
  for data in yaml.load_all(f):
    if data is not None and data['kind'] == 'Deployment':
      dep = data.copy()
k8s_beta = client.ExtensionsV1beta1Api()
dep['metadata']['annotations'] = {
  'kubernetes.io/change-cause': 'kubectl set image deployment/{0} {0}={0}:{1} --record'.format("your-deployment-name", "tag-of-your-image")
}
resp = k8s_beta.patch_namespaced_deployment(name="your-deployment-name"],
                                                    body=dep, namespace="default")
...
```

Using docker there is many logs running `docker logs localstack_localstack_1` where localstack_localstack_1 is docker instance name

For lambda triggered asynchronously (by SNS for example) logs are not in docker logs of localstack image, but can be found with a trick:
Run localstack with `LAMBDA_EXECUTOR=docker`
Each time a lambda is triggered it creates a new docker instance which can be found using `docker ps -a`
```
$ docker ps -a
CONTAINER ID        IMAGE                                               COMMAND                  CREATED             STATUS                      PORTS                                                                                                                      NAMES
ac7a9a146302        lambci/lambda:nodejs6.10                            "/var/lang/bin/nod..."   22 minutes ago      Exited (1) 21 minutes ago                                                                                                                              distracted_williams

I understand what you are trying to achieve here and agree that it can be done easily with TF. What bothers me here is that the computation of an operation to perform relies on information about its outputs. This is like overloading by return types in C/C++ and should be avoided.

That is to say, just the number of outputs specified alone can actually change the behavior of the algorithm. This seems like a bad idea. Output shapes/types can be used to ensure correctness (like type checking), but should not be used to determine computation. This is conceptually true for most programming languages like C/C++ in which return type information of a function alone cannot determine/change the behavior of the function. Even if that is the default behavior of PyTorch/Caffe, I believe we should make it right here. 

Specifically, Tensorflow split makes a bit more sense (although arguably hacky nontheless):
https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/split. We can pass that `n` you mentioned as the `num_or_size_splits` argument. I'd propose to modify the Spec for split to make room for another argument (or extend the purpose of the split argument to be like TF).
